finding: Subjective value function becomes step-like (insensitive to magnitude changes) when affective cues dominate preference formation, versus remaining proportional when quantitative reasoning predominates; the same stimuli produce different value sensitivity patterns depending on which cognitive process is engaged.

Initial exertion of executive control temporarily reduces capacity for subsequent unrelated executive control; the effect is specific to effortful control processes (not general fatigue or difficulty) and dissociates: inhibition and updating share depleting resources, but simple maintenance does not.

Future-directed mental time travel produces more intense emotional arousal than past-directed time travel for identical events; this anticipation-advantage persists even for purely hypothetical events, suggesting the advantage arises from processing differences in simulation extent rather than uncertainty alone.

Subjective experiences of processing difficulty (disfluency) shift reasoning from intuitive heuristics to analytic deliberation; the shift occurs via metacognitive inference that difficult-to-process information signals a task requiring more careful thought, not merely from stimulus-induced processing speed changes.

Repetition priming in lexical decision separates into two mechanisms: a masked repetition effect (frequency-insensitive, brief) reflecting pure lexical activation, and a longer-term episodic effect (frequency-attenuated) appearing only when subjects make task-relevant responses to primes; frequency attenuation is entirely episodic.

Spacing effects in memory dissociate by task type: cued-memory tasks (recognition, frequency judgment) show spacing benefits only for intentionally learned items via differential rehearsal; free recall shows spacing benefits for both intentional and incidental learning via automatic encoding of temporal context information.
